name: CRREM EU Risk Assessment
description: Paris-aligned stranding assessment for commercial and residential buildings across 30 European jurisdictions.
longDescription: The Carbon Risk Real Estate Monitor (CRREM) translates global carbon budgets into property-level carbon intensity and energy use intensity (EUI) pathways from 2020 to 2050. For each building, it calculates a projected emissions trajectory based on reported energy consumption and country-specific grid decarbonization trends, then compares this against the science-based 1.5°C decarbonization pathway to determine stranding year, excess emissions, and financial risk exposure.
author: CRREM Foundation / IIÖ Institut für Immobilienökonomie
scenario: 1.5°C (IEA NZE 2050); 2°C alternative; RCP 4.5 / 8.5 for climate projections
scope: Whole-building operational GHG (Scope 1 + 2 + downstream tenant energy); CO2e including F-gases; embodied carbon excluded
limitations: Embodied carbon excluded — operational energy only
Location-based accounting only — RECs/GOs not reflected in grid emission factors
Static fuel emission factors for natural gas and fuel oil (only grid electricity and DH projected forward)
Single reporting-year baseline — no multi-year trending
No building-level retrofit feasibility validation
Data centres explicitly excluded (EUI too high)
V2.07 is final Excel release — CRREM transitioning to disaggregated open-access data
citation: CRREM Foundation / IIO (2025). CRREM Risk Assessment Tool V2.07. Carbon Risk Real Estate Monitor. https://www.crrem.eu

inputs.label: Property Type
inputs.description: OFF, RHS, RSM, RWB, DWC, DWW, HOT, HEC, LEI, RMF

outputs.label: Stranding Year
outputs.description: First year asset CI exceeds CRREM pathway

outputs.label: Baseline Carbon Intensity
outputs.description: From reported energy and emission factors

outputs.label: Baseline EUI
outputs.description: Energy use intensity at floor area

outputs.label: NPV Excess Costs
outputs.description: Net present value at 3% discount rate

outputs.label: Carbon Value at Risk
outputs.description: NPV costs as share of gross asset value

outputs.label: Renewable Share
outputs.description: On-site renewables as % of total energy

name: CRREM North America
description: Stranding assessment for US and Canadian buildings across 57 US sub-regions (eGRID × CBECS climate zone) and 11 Canadian provinces.
longDescription: The North America edition of CRREM extends the science-based 1.5°C decarbonization framework to the US and Canada, developed in partnership with Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory (LBNL) and the ULI Greenprint Center. It uses eGRID sub-regions cross-tabulated with CBECS climate zones to create 57 granular US sub-regions, plus 11 Canadian provinces/territories, each with dedicated carbon intensity and EUI pathways. Grid emission factor projections use EPA Cambium data, and baseline EUIs are derived from CBECS (US) and NRCan surveys (Canada).
author: CRREM Foundation, Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory (LBNL), ULI Greenprint
scenario: 1.5°C (IEA NZE 2050); 2°C alternative; RCP 4.5 / 8.5 for climate projections
scope: Whole-building operational GHG (Scope 1 + 2 + downstream tenant energy); CO2e including F-gases; embodied carbon excluded
limitations: US CBECS baselines represent buildings averaging ~16,300 sq ft — smaller than typical institutional assets
Canada excludes Small Multi-family property type (15 of 16 types only)
Canada uses sector-level weighted emission factors (less granular than US)
Alaska and Hawaii use national Cambium shape applied to local starting values
Building-level renewable energy procurement does not alter pathway performance
Embodied carbon excluded from pathways
citation: CRREM Foundation / LBNL (2025). CRREM Risk Assessment Tool — North America, V2.07. https://crrem.org

name: CRREM Asia-Pacific
description: Stranding assessment for buildings across 9 Asia-Pacific jurisdictions including Australia, China, Hong Kong, India, Japan, Malaysia, Philippines, Singapore, and South Korea.
longDescription: The Asia-Pacific edition of CRREM applies the science-based 1.5°C decarbonization framework to 9 major APAC markets. Like the EU and NA editions, it calculates property-level carbon intensity and EUI pathways from 2020 to 2050, using jurisdiction-specific grid emission factors, district heating factors, and climate projections (HDD/CDD). The APAC pathways account for the region's diverse energy mixes and rapid urbanization, with country-specific baseline EUIs and grid decarbonization trajectories.
author: CRREM Foundation / IIÖ Institut für Immobilienökonomie
scenario: 1.5°C (IEA NZE 2050); 2°C alternative; RCP 4.5 / 8.5 for climate projections
scope: Whole-building operational GHG (Scope 1 + 2 + downstream tenant energy); CO2e including F-gases; embodied carbon excluded
limitations: Grid emission factor data quality varies across APAC jurisdictions
Fewer country-specific validation studies than EU edition
Embodied carbon excluded from pathways
Building-level renewable energy procurement does not alter pathway performance
citation: CRREM Foundation / IIO (2025). CRREM Risk Assessment Tool — Asia-Pacific, V2.07. https://crrem.org

name: PCAF Global GHG Standard
description: GHG accounting and reporting standard for financial institutions — measures financed emissions across 10 asset classes including commercial real estate and mortgages.
longDescription: The PCAF Global GHG Accounting and Reporting Standard provides a harmonized methodology for financial institutions to measure, report, and disclose greenhouse gas emissions associated with their lending and investment portfolios (Scope 3, Category 15). For real estate, PCAF covers two asset classes: Commercial Real Estate (offices, retail, hotels, industrial) and Mortgages (residential). Building-level Scope 1 and 2 emissions are attributed to the financial institution based on the ratio of loan/investment amount to property value. Data quality is scored 1–5, where Score 1 represents verified building-level data and Score 5 represents floor-area-based estimates. The standard is in conformance with the GHG Protocol and referenced by TCFD, CDP, CSRD, SFDR, and the Net-Zero Banking Alliance.
author: Partnership for Carbon Accounting Financials (PCAF)
scope: Scope 3 Category 15 (financed emissions); attributes Scope 1 + 2 of borrowers/investees; 10 asset classes
limitations: Data quality is primary challenge — most portfolios rely on estimated data (scores 3–5)
~35% of properties typically lack EPC data, forcing modeled estimates
Attribution approach risks double-counting across multiple financiers
PCAF states estimates may not be useful for individual investment decisions
Scope 3 of underlying borrowers/investees is extremely difficult to measure
Part B (Facilitated Emissions) still under development
citation: PCAF (2025). The Global GHG Accounting and Reporting Standard for the Financial Industry. Part A: Financed Emissions. Third Edition. https://carbonaccountingfinancials.com/standard

name: SBTi for Buildings (via CRREM)
description: Science-based target-setting for real estate using CRREM-aligned 1.5°C pathways. Covers in-use operational emissions and embodied carbon with a whole-building approach.
longDescription: The SBTi Buildings Sector Guidance provides a standardized methodology for companies in the buildings sector to set greenhouse gas reduction targets aligned with 1.5°C. It uses a Sectoral Decarbonization Approach (SDA) that translates global carbon budgets into region- and property-type-specific pathways. For in-use operational emissions, the framework uses CRREM-aligned pathways co-developed in a formal partnership since January 2022. For embodied emissions, pathways were developed with Ramboll. The framework applies a whole-building approach covering landlord and tenant energy across Scope 1, 2, and 3, and is used by over 10,000 companies globally.
author: Science Based Targets initiative (SBTi) — CDP, UNGC, WRI, WWF
scenario: 1.5°C (Sectoral Decarbonization Approach via IEA NZE / IPCC AR6)
scope: Whole-building: Scope 1 + 2 + Scope 3 (tenant energy); in-use operational + embodied carbon (new developments)
limitations: Performance gap — modeled vs. actual energy consumption can diverge significantly
Scope 3 tenant data is difficult to obtain
Only 29% of pathways were pilot-tested with real-world data before release
Embodied emissions methodology is newer and less mature than operational pathways
Near-term targets only (5–10yr); long-term net-zero guidance is separate
Infrastructure explicitly excluded from Buildings Criteria
citation: SBTi (2025). Buildings Sector Science-Based Target-Setting Criteria, Version 1.1. https://sciencebasedtargets.org/sectors/buildings

name: Architecture 2030 Zero Tool
description: Building energy benchmarking against zero-carbon targets. Normalizes by climate, space type, size, occupancy, and schedule using CBECS baselines.
longDescription: The Zero Tool by Architecture 2030 calculates a building's energy consumption baseline and target, producing a score where 0 equals a Zero Net Carbon building. It normalizes performance using climate zone, building type, gross area, occupancy levels, and operating schedule. Baseline data comes from CBECS 2003, and the tool aligns with the Zero Energy Performance Index (zEPI) and HERS Index. A free API and portfolio bulk upload are available.
author: Architecture 2030
scope: Operational energy (site and source EUI)
limitations: CBECS 2003 baselines may not reflect current building stock
US/Canada focus — limited international applicability
Does not account for embodied carbon
citation: Architecture 2030 (2024). Zero Tool. https://www.zerotool.org/

name: EC3 Embodied Carbon Calculator
description: Benchmarking and reducing embodied carbon in construction materials using the world's largest EPD database. Open source with open API.
longDescription: EC3 (Embodied Carbon in Construction Calculator) by Building Transparency is a free, open-access tool for comparing and reducing the embodied carbon of construction materials. It houses the world's largest database of Environmental Product Declarations (EPDs) and enables material comparison during specification and procurement. Projects using EC3 have reported 30%+ reductions in embodied carbon. The tool provides building-level and material-level carbon benchmarking.
author: Building Transparency (501c3 nonprofit)
scope: Upfront embodied carbon (A1–A3 manufacturing); cradle-to-gate EPD data
limitations: EPD coverage varies by material category and region
Focuses on upfront embodied carbon (A1–A3); operational carbon not covered
EPD quality and comparability can vary between PCRs
citation: Building Transparency (2024). EC3: Embodied Carbon in Construction Calculator. https://www.buildingtransparency.org/tools/ec3/

name: EU Level(s) Framework
description: European Commission framework with 16 sustainability indicators across carbon, energy, water, materials, health, comfort, and climate resilience for buildings.
longDescription: Level(s) is the European Commission's voluntary framework for sustainable buildings, developed by the Joint Research Centre. It defines 16 indicators across 6 macro-objectives: greenhouse gas emissions, resource efficiency (energy, water, materials), health and comfort, climate resilience, and life cycle cost. Each indicator can be assessed at three levels of detail, from simple design-stage estimates to as-built verified performance. Level(s) is referenced in the EU Taxonomy for sustainable activities and supports compliance with CSRD and the Energy Performance of Buildings Directive (EPBD).
author: European Commission (Joint Research Centre)
scope: Whole lifecycle: operational energy, embodied carbon, water, waste, health, comfort, resilience, cost
limitations: EU residential and office focus — limited property type coverage
Three assessment levels add complexity for full compliance
Not a certification — a measurement framework only
citation: European Commission (2024). Level(s) — European framework for sustainable buildings. https://green-forum.ec.europa.eu/green-business/levels_en

name: ASHRAE Building EQ
description: Building energy performance scoring using metered data or ASHRAE 90.1 simulation. Compares actual EUI against Standard 100 baselines.
longDescription: ASHRAE Building EQ (Energy Quotient) benchmarks building energy performance using two methods: 'In Operation' compares actual metered energy data against ASHRAE Standard 100 baselines or Architecture 2030 Zero Tool references; 'As Designed' uses ASHRAE 90.1 Appendix G simulation to rate design-stage performance. The scoring formula is straightforward: (EUI_actual / EUI_baseline) × 100, making it easily implementable as a web calculator.
author: ASHRAE
scope: Operational energy (site and source EUI)
limitations: Baseline data primarily from US CBECS
Scoring methodology documented but tool requires ASHRAE account
Does not cover carbon intensity — energy only
citation: ASHRAE (2024). Building Energy Quotient (Building EQ). https://www.ashrae.org/technical-resources/building-eq

name: OS-Climate physrisk
description: Open-source physical climate risk analysis for real estate portfolios. Models flood, heat stress, wind, and drought impacts on individual assets.
longDescription: OS-Climate physrisk is an open-source Python library developed under the Linux Foundation (FINOS), led by BNP Paribas. It provides physical climate risk analysis for large portfolios of individual assets, modeling the impact of climate hazards including riverine and coastal flooding, extreme heat, tropical cyclones, and drought. Designed for real estate mortgage portfolios, credit risk assessment, and loan origination, it uses CMIP6 climate projections and geospatial hazard data to produce asset-level risk scores.
author: OS-Climate / Linux Foundation (FINOS), led by BNP Paribas
scope: Physical climate hazards: flood, heat, wind, drought — economic damage estimation
limitations: Python library requires backend service or JS port for web use
Hazard model resolution varies by data source
Economic damage functions are generalized — not building-specific
Actively under development — API may change
citation: OS-Climate (2025). physrisk: Physical Climate Risk Analysis. https://github.com/os-climate/physrisk

name: IFC Building Resilience Index
description: Web-based tool that evaluates location-specific climate and disaster risks for buildings, assigning an A+/A/B/C/R resilience grade.
longDescription: The IFC Building Resilience Index (BRI), developed by the International Finance Corporation (World Bank Group), is a web-based self-assessment tool that evaluates a building's exposure to climate hazards and natural disasters. It assigns a standardized grade (A+/A/B/C/R) based on location-specific risk exposure and the adaptation measures implemented. The tool is designed for emerging markets and supports investors, developers, and building owners in understanding and communicating physical climate resilience.
author: IFC (World Bank Group)
scope: Physical resilience: hazard exposure + adaptation measures → A+/A/B/C/R grade
limitations: Originally self-declaration; third-party verification now available via Bureau Veritas (since July 2025)
Emerging market focus may limit developed-market applicability
Grade reflects both exposure and adaptation — not pure risk
citation: IFC (2024). Building Resilience Index. https://www.resilienceindex.org/

name: ENERGY STAR Portfolio Manager
description: US EPA tool that benchmarks building energy performance against national averages. Produces a 1–100 score where 50 is the median for similar buildings.
longDescription: ENERGY STAR Portfolio Manager is the US EPA's free online tool for benchmarking building energy and water performance. It compares a building's actual utility data against the national median for similar building types derived from CBECS, producing a 1–100 score where 50 represents the median. Buildings scoring 75 or above qualify for ENERGY STAR certification. The tool calculates Site EUI, Source EUI, and weather-normalized metrics. A full RESTful web services API enables data exchange with third-party platforms.
author: US Environmental Protection Agency (EPA)
scope: Operational energy and water consumption
limitations: US-only — scoring uses CBECS data for US buildings
Score available for ~20 eligible building types; others get EUI only
Does not measure carbon intensity directly
citation: US EPA (2025). ENERGY STAR Portfolio Manager. https://www.energystar.gov/buildings/benchmark

name: NGFS Climate Scenarios
description: Comprehensive climate scenario data for transition and physical risks from the Network for Greening the Financial System. Used by central banks worldwide.
longDescription: The NGFS Climate Scenarios, produced by the Network for Greening the Financial System (a consortium of 140+ central banks and supervisors), provide comprehensive scenario data for both transition and physical climate risks. Version 5.0 includes macroeconomic impact projections, carbon price trajectories, energy mix evolution, and subnational physical risk indicators via the Climate Impact Explorer. The scenarios span Orderly, Disorderly, and Hot House World narratives, enabling financial institutions to stress-test portfolios against different climate futures.
author: Network for Greening the Financial System (NGFS)
scenario: Net Zero 2050, Low Demand, Below 2°C, Delayed Transition, Fragmented World, NDCs, Current Policies
scope: Transition risk (carbon prices, energy mix, GDP) + physical risk (chronic and acute hazards)
limitations: Macro-level scenarios — building-level translation requires additional modeling
Physical risk indicators are subnational but not property-level
Scenario narratives are illustrative — not forecasts
citation: NGFS (2024). NGFS Climate Scenarios, Version 5.0. https://www.ngfs.net/ngfs-scenarios-portal/

name: NABERS
description: Australian government rating system measuring operational environmental performance of buildings using actual utility data. 0–6 star scale.
longDescription: NABERS (National Australian Built Environment Rating System) is the Australian government's rating system for measuring the operational environmental performance of buildings. It uses actual utility consumption data to produce a 0–6 star rating normalized for climate zone, hours of operation, and building type. NABERS covers energy, water, waste, and indoor environment quality. The energy rating methodology is well-documented with clear normalization formulas, and NABERS ratings are mandatory for commercial office buildings over 1,000 m² in Australia under the Commercial Building Disclosure program.
author: NSW Government (DCCEEW) on behalf of Australian Government
scope: Operational energy, water, waste, indoor environment
limitations: Australia only — not directly applicable to other jurisdictions
Official rating requires an accredited NABERS assessor
Star rating bands are periodically recalibrated
citation: Australian Government (2025). NABERS — National Australian Built Environment Rating System. https://www.nabers.gov.au/

name: IFC EDGE
description: Free web app that calculates resource-efficient building design strategies for energy, water, and embodied carbon. Targets 20%+ savings in 170+ countries.
longDescription: EDGE (Excellence in Design for Greater Efficiencies), developed by the IFC (World Bank Group) and certified by GBCI, is a free web application for calculating optimal resource-efficient building design. It compares energy, water, and embodied carbon performance against country-specific baselines, targeting a minimum 20% improvement. EDGE covers 170+ countries with localized climate and construction data, making it the leading green building tool for emerging markets. Three certification levels are available: EDGE Certified (20%), EDGE Advanced (40%), and Zero Carbon.
author: IFC (World Bank Group), certified by GBCI
scope: Operational energy, water consumption, and embodied carbon of materials
limitations: Design-stage tool — does not track as-built or operational performance
Country-specific baselines may not reflect local market conditions
Certification requires third-party audit (paid)
citation: IFC (2025). EDGE — Excellence in Design for Greater Efficiencies, v3.1. https://edgebuildings.com/

name: BAFU CO2 Calculator for Buildings
description: Swiss federal building-level CO2 emissions calculator using the GWR register and SIA 380/1 standard. Simulates heating system replacements and energy retrofits.
longDescription: The CO2-Rechner Gebäude is an interactive tool developed for the Swiss Federal Office for the Environment (BAFU/FOEN) that provides climate and energy-relevant information for every residential building in Switzerland. It calculates CO2 emissions under standard conditions based on data from the Federal Register of Buildings and Dwellings (GWR), incorporating heating system type, energy source, building use, heated area, year of construction, altitude, and topographical location. Calculations follow SIA Norm 380/1 (2016). Users can simulate how a change of energy source (e.g., from gas to heat pump) or an energy-efficient renovation would improve the CO2 value. The 2024 expanded version extends coverage to Scope 2 emissions and provides rough estimates of embodied carbon from building materials. Deployed as part of the PACTA Climate Test for Swiss financial institutions.
author: Swiss Federal Office for the Environment (BAFU/FOEN), developed by Wüest Partner AG

license.name: Open source (R script, available on request)

scenario: Swiss Net-Zero 2050 (Climate and Innovation Act / CO2 Act)
scope: Scope 1 (heating fuels) + Scope 2 (electricity, district heating); rough embodied carbon estimates
limitations: Standard values only — SIA 380/1 demand values may not reflect actual consumption
Residential focus — commercial building coverage is limited
GWR data may have incomplete or outdated entries
Embodied carbon estimates are approximate — not full lifecycle assessments
Switzerland-only — depends on Swiss-specific data sources (GWR, EGID, SIA)
No actual metering — does not incorporate smart meter data
citation: BAFU (2024). Methodenbericht 2024 zum erweiterten CO2-Rechner für Gebäude. Bundesamt für Umwelt, Bern.

name: CLIMADA
description: Open-source probabilistic multi-hazard climate risk platform. Models tropical cyclones, floods, droughts, heat, windstorms, and wildfire with economic damage estimation.
longDescription: CLIMADA (CLIMate ADAptation) is an open-source probabilistic risk assessment framework developed by ETH Zurich. It integrates hazard, exposure, and vulnerability data to estimate economic damages from natural catastrophes and evaluate adaptation options. Covers tropical cyclones, European windstorms, river floods, wildfire, hail, and coastal flooding. Used by EIOPA for EU insurance supervision. A data API provides global hazard layers at ~4km resolution.
author: Weather and Climate Risks Group, ETH Zurich
scope: Economic damage from natural hazards: wind, flood, drought, heat, wildfire
limitations: Python library requires backend service for web use
4km default resolution may miss local effects
Damage functions are generalized, not building-specific
citation: Aznar-Siguan, G. and Bresch, D.N. (2019). CLIMADA v1. Geosci. Model Dev., 12, 3085-3097.

name: OpenQuake Engine
description: Gold-standard open-source engine for probabilistic seismic hazard and risk assessment at building level.
longDescription: OpenQuake is the benchmark open-source software for earthquake risk assessment by the GEM Foundation. Computes ground shaking hazard maps, building damage, and economic losses. Includes Global Exposure Model (200+ countries), Global Vulnerability Model (3,500+ typologies), and Global Seismic Hazard Map.
author: Global Earthquake Model (GEM) Foundation
scope: Seismic hazard curves, building damage, economic loss, loss exceedance curves
limitations: Earthquake-only
Requires seismic source models per region
Complex vulnerability function setup
citation: Pagani, M. et al. (2014). OpenQuake Engine. Seismological Research Letters, 85(3), 692-702.

name: BETTER
description: Open-source tool identifying cost-effective energy efficiency measures for buildings with ranked retrofit recommendations.
longDescription: BETTER (Building Efficiency Targeting Tool for Energy Retrofits) by LBNL identifies cost-effective energy efficiency measures using readily available building and energy data. Calculates savings potential, ranks measures by cost-effectiveness, and generates actionable recommendations.
author: Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory (LBNL), Johnson Controls, ICF
scope: Operational energy savings potential, retrofit measure ranking
limitations: US-focused cost defaults
Commercial buildings only
No embodied carbon of retrofits
citation: LBNL (2024). BETTER. https://better.lbl.gov/

name: TABULA/EPISCOPE
description: Residential building typologies for 20 European countries with energy data for typical, standard, and advanced refurbishment states.
longDescription: TABULA/EPISCOPE provides harmonized residential building typology database for 20 European countries under the EU IEE programme. Web tool calculates heating demand, energy consumption, and CO2 emissions based on building age, type, and country-specific archetypes.
author: Institut Wohnen und Umwelt (IWU), Germany
scope: Heating demand, energy use, CO2 emissions for residential archetypes
limitations: Residential only
Data from 2016
20 EU countries (not all 27)
citation: Loga, T. et al. (2016). TABULA building typologies. Energy and Buildings, 132, 4-12.

name: SFINCS
description: Fast compound flooding model from Deltares. Simulates coastal surge, riverine, and rainfall flooding 100–1000x faster than full hydrodynamic models.
longDescription: SFINCS (Super-Fast INundation of CoastS) by Deltares simulates compound flooding from coastal surge, riverine overflow, and rainfall. Runs 100–1000x faster than full 2D models. Validated globally, integrated with FloodAdapt.
author: Deltares (Netherlands)
scope: Flood inundation depth and extent from compound flooding
limitations: Reduced complexity vs full 2D models
Requires DEM data
No built-in damage functions
citation: Leijnse, T. et al. (2021). SFINCS. Coastal Engineering, 163, 103796.

name: WRI Aqueduct
description: Global water risk atlas mapping 13 indicators including water stress, drought risk, and groundwater depletion with CMIP6 projections.
longDescription: WRI Aqueduct maps 13 water risk indicators using PCR-GLOBWB 2 hydrological model with CMIP6 projections to 2080. Standard tool for TCFD water risk disclosure.
author: World Resources Institute + Utrecht University
scope: Water stress, drought risk, groundwater depletion
limitations: Sub-basin resolution, not property-level
No building-level impact modeling
citation: Kuzma, S. et al. (2023). Aqueduct 4.0: Updated Decision-Relevant Global Water Risk Indicators. WRI Technical Note.

name: Climate Bonds Buildings Criteria
description: Green bond certification screening for buildings with free CO2 calculators. Top-15% carbon intensity targets per city.
longDescription: Climate Bonds Initiative defines carbon performance thresholds for green bond certification. Targets represent top 15% per city/typology with zero-carbon 2050 trajectory. Free CO2 calculators. Aligned with EU Taxonomy.
author: Climate Bonds Initiative (CBI)
scope: Building carbon intensity screening for green bond eligibility
limitations: Screening only, not building-level calculation
City-specific targets incomplete
Certification is paid
citation: CBI (2023). Buildings Criteria. https://www.climatebonds.net/

name: eLCA + ÖKOBAUDAT
description: Free open-source building LCA tool using Germany’s standardized EPD database. Whole-lifecycle impacts per EN 15978.
longDescription: eLCA is a free open-source online building LCA tool by BBSR using the ÖKOBAUDAT EPD database. Calculates GWP, AP, EP, ODP, POCP per EN 15978. Mandatory for German BNB federal buildings.
author: BBSR (Germany)
scope: Whole-lifecycle environmental impacts per EN 15978
limitations: German-focused EPD data
Interface in German
Requires detailed material input
citation: BBSR (2025). eLCA. https://www.bauteileditor.de

name: Smart Readiness Indicator (SRI)
description: Official EU metric rating building smart readiness across 9 technical domains for energy optimization and grid flexibility.
longDescription: The SRI rates buildings across 9 domains (heating, cooling, DHW, ventilation, lighting, envelope, electricity, EV charging, monitoring) per EU Delegated Regulation 2020/2155 and EPBD 2024 recast.
author: European Commission / Fraunhofer IBP / VITO
scope: Smart readiness scoring across 9 technical domains
limitations: Optional for member states
Methodology still evolving
No direct energy measurement
citation: EC (2020). Delegated Regulation (EU) 2020/2155.

name: DGNB System
description: German multi-criteria sustainability certification. 29 criteria, 6 quality areas, Bronze–Platinum ratings, full lifecycle.
longDescription: DGNB System evaluates buildings across 29 criteria in 6 areas: environmental, economic, sociocultural, technical, process, and site quality. International version covers 30+ countries. Aligned with EU Taxonomy.
author: Deutsche Gesellschaft für Nachhaltiges Bauen (DGNB)
scope: 38 criteria across 6 quality areas
limitations: Requires licensed auditor
German-origin
Full scoring needs DGNB tools
citation: DGNB (2023). DGNB System New Construction, Version 2023.

name: BREEAM
description: World’s most widely used sustainability assessment. V7 maps to EU Taxonomy. Pass through Outstanding.
longDescription: BREEAM evaluates management, energy, water, transport, health, materials, waste, pollution, land use, and ecology. V7 (2024) maps to EU Taxonomy. Used in 100+ countries.
author: BRE Group (UK)
scope: 10 categories including energy, materials, water, health, ecology
limitations: Requires licensed assessor
Scheme docs for purchase
Different schemes per building stage
citation: BRE (2025). BREEAM New Construction V7. https://breeam.com/

name: FEMA HAZUS / FAST
description: US multi-hazard loss estimation. FAST is open-source Python for building-level flood damage at ~10,000 structures/second.
longDescription: HAZUS estimates building damages and economic losses from floods, hurricanes, earthquakes. FAST is the open-source Python tool calculating building-specific flood losses using depth-damage functions.
author: FEMA / National Institute of Building Sciences
scope: Building damage, economic loss from flood/hurricane/earthquake
limitations: US only
Full HAZUS requires ArcGIS
Default inventory is census-tract level
citation: FEMA (2025). Hazus 7.1. https://www.fema.gov/flood-maps/products-tools/hazus

name: JRC Flood Hazard Maps
description: Pre-computed flood maps for 10–500yr return periods. 25m EU, 90m global. Open data from Copernicus.
longDescription: JRC Global and European River Flood Hazard Maps provide inundation extent and depth for return periods from 10 to 500 years. Derived from LISFLOOD model. Available via Copernicus EMS and Google Earth Engine.
author: European Commission Joint Research Centre (JRC)
scope: Riverine flood inundation extent and depth
limitations: Riverine only, no pluvial/coastal
No climate change projections
90m global resolution
citation: Dottori, F. et al. (2022). JRC Global River Flood Hazard Maps.

name: EIOPA CLIMADA-App
description: GUI wrapper around CLIMADA for European insurers. Run NatCat loss scenarios for 30 countries without coding.
longDescription: EIOPA CLIMADA-App is a GUI built on CLIMADA by EIOPA and ETH Zurich for non-technical users to run probabilistic NatCat scenarios for European countries without Python.
author: EIOPA + ETH Zurich
scope: NatCat loss scenarios for European insurance portfolios
limitations: Europe only (30 countries)
Simplified vs full CLIMADA
Inherits CLIMADA resolution limits
citation: EIOPA (2024). Open-Source Climate Tools.

name: Oasis Loss Modelling Framework
description: Open-source cat modelling platform. 100+ models from 21+ providers. Industry standard for insurers and reinsurers.
longDescription: Oasis LMF is the open-source catastrophe modelling platform with standardized API, data formats, and Monte Carlo engine. 100+ models from 21+ providers. Web UI, REST API, Docker deployment.
author: Oasis LMF Ltd (insurance consortium)
scope: Cat model execution: ground-up loss, insured loss, reinsurance loss
limitations: Platform only — peril models licensed separately
Insurance-focused data structures
Complex setup
citation: Oasis LMF (2026). https://oasislmf.org/

name: CBE Thermal Comfort Tool
description: Free online thermal comfort calculator implementing ASHRAE 55, ISO 7730, and EN 16798. Calculates PMV/PPD, adaptive comfort, and SET.
longDescription: The CBE Thermal Comfort Tool from UC Berkeley calculates thermal comfort indices from ASHRAE 55-2023, ISO 7730:2005, and EN 16798-1:2022. Provides PMV/PPD, adaptive comfort, Standard Effective Temperature, local discomfort, and SolarCal calculations with psychrometric chart visualizations. ~50,000 annual users.
author: Center for the Built Environment (CBE), UC Berkeley
scope: Thermal comfort: PMV, PPD, adaptive comfort, SET, UTCI, local discomfort
limitations: Steady-state models only (no transient response)
Single-zone calculation (no room-level spatial variation)
Requires measured environmental parameters as input
citation: Tartarini, F. et al. (2020). CBE Thermal Comfort Tool. SoftwareX, 12, 100563.

name: REopt
description: Techno-economic optimization platform for solar, battery, wind, and CHP at buildings and campuses. Calculates optimal sizing, dispatch, and financial returns.
longDescription: REopt by NREL uses mixed-integer linear programming to optimize the mix of solar PV, wind, battery storage, CHP, and thermal storage for buildings and campuses. Calculates dispatch strategies, net present value, payback period, and grid outage resilience. Supported 260+ MW of renewable energy decisions.
author: National Renewable Energy Laboratory (NREL), US DOE
scope: Optimal DER sizing, dispatch, NPV, payback, resilience, emissions reduction
limitations: US-focused utility rate structures and incentive databases
Requires detailed load profile data
Linear programming may not capture all real-world constraints
citation: NREL (2025). REopt: Renewable Energy Integration and Optimization. https://reopt.nrel.gov/

name: pyBuildingEnergy
description: Open-source Python library implementing ISO 52016-1:2018 for EPBD-compliant building energy performance calculation.
longDescription: pyBuildingEnergy by EURAC Research implements the ISO 52016-1:2018 standard for building energy performance assessment aligned with EPBD requirements. Calculates energy need for heating/cooling (hourly and monthly), internal temperature profiles, and sensible heating/cooling loads. Future modules planned for ventilation, renewables, and building systems.
author: EURAC Research, Institute for Renewable Energy (Bolzano, Italy)
scope: Energy need for heating/cooling per ISO 52016-1, internal temperatures, loads
limitations: Heating/cooling only (no ventilation, renewables, or systems yet)
Academic origin, limited production testing
Requires detailed building geometry and construction input
citation: EURAC Research (2024). pyBuildingEnergy. https://github.com/EURAC-EEBgroup/pyBuildingEnergy

name: FlexMeasures
description: Open-source energy management system for optimizing behind-the-meter flexibility. Schedules batteries, heat pumps, EVs, and industrial processes.
longDescription: FlexMeasures, contributed to LF Energy (Linux Foundation), turns energy data into optimized schedules for batteries, heat pumps, EVs, and flexible processes. Handles forecasting, scheduling, and real-time dispatch. Well-documented REST API and CLI, extensible via plugins.
author: Seita Energy Flexibility / LF Energy (Linux Foundation)
scope: Optimal dispatch schedules for flexible assets, flexibility value quantification
limitations: Requires real-time or forecast data feeds
Primarily behind-the-meter focus
Complex setup for full deployment
citation: FlexMeasures (2025). https://flexmeasures.io/

name: ResStock / ComStock
description: Large-scale US building stock energy modeling with retrofit cost data. ResStock covers residential, ComStock covers commercial. Open source.
longDescription: ResStock and ComStock by NREL use OpenStudio/EnergyPlus to simulate energy use and retrofit scenarios across the entire US building stock. Include cost data for energy efficiency measures. ResStock has identified $49 billion in potential annual utility bill savings. Results available at state and county level.
author: National Renewable Energy Laboratory (NREL), US DOE
scope: Building stock energy use, retrofit measure costs and savings, utility bill impacts
limitations: US only (CBECS/RECS building stock)
Simulated, not measured performance
Aggregate results, not individual building assessment
citation: Wilson, E. et al. (2022). ResStock. NREL. https://resstock.nrel.gov/

name: DER-VET
description: Open-source tool for optimizing sizing and dispatch of battery storage, solar, wind, and other DERs. Evaluates grid service revenue streams.
longDescription: DER-VET (Distributed Energy Resource Value Estimation Tool) by EPRI optimizes the sizing and dispatch of battery storage, solar PV, wind, demand response, EV charging, CHP, and other DERs. Evaluates benefits and costs across grid services including demand charge reduction, time-of-use arbitrage, frequency regulation, and capacity markets.
author: Electric Power Research Institute (EPRI)
scope: DER sizing, dispatch, revenue from grid services, cost-benefit analysis
limitations: US market structures and tariffs
Requires detailed tariff and load data
Linear optimization may simplify real dispatch
citation: EPRI (2024). DER-VET. https://www.der-vet.com/

name: SNBS Hochbau
description: Swiss multi-criteria sustainability certification for buildings. 35 criteria across society, economy, and environment with Silver/Gold/Platinum ratings.
longDescription: The Swiss Sustainable Building Standard (SNBS-Hochbau) provides a comprehensive framework for assessing building sustainability across three equally weighted pillars — society, economy, and environment. It evaluates 35 criteria organized into 11 themes using 98 measurement parameters. Buildings are scored on a 1–6 scale and may achieve Platinum (5.5–6.0), Gold (5.0–5.4), or Silver (4.0–4.9) certification. Since November 2023, SNBS shares a common label platform with Minergie, enabling streamlined dual certification.
author: NNBS (Netzwerk Nachhaltiges Bauen Schweiz), operated by Minergie
scope: Full triple-bottom-line: operational energy, embodied carbon, indoor environment, social sustainability, lifecycle costs, biodiversity, water
limitations: Switzerland-only — tailored to Swiss regulations and SIA norms
Limited to residential, office, and educational buildings
No financial risk output (certification only, not stranding/CVaR)
Energy targets reference 2000-Watt Society, not IPCC 1.5°C scenarios
Fewer indicators than DGNB (98 vs 343 parameters)
Qualitative architectural assessment introduces evaluator subjectivity
citation: NNBS (2023). Kriterienbeschrieb SNBS-Hochbau, Version 2023.1. https://www.snbs-hochbau.ch/

name: geo.admin.ch Hazard Layers
description: Swiss federal geoportal with 50+ coordinate-queryable layers for CO2 emissions, natural hazards, building data, solar potential, and environmental risk.
longDescription: The geo.admin.ch REST API provides access to 50+ layers relevant to real estate climate risk, all queryable per-coordinate. Key layers include: CO2 emissions per building (BAFU), flood zones at 4 return periods (Aquaprotect), earthquake zones (SIA 261), storm gust peaks at 4 return periods, hail hazard by grain size, building register (GWR with 100+ fields), Minergie certifications, solar roof/facade potential, district heating networks, surface runoff hazard, radon probability, and noise exposure.
author: swisstopo / BAFU / BFE / MeteoSwiss
scenario: Multiple return periods (50/100/250/500yr for flood, 10/20/50/100yr for hail)
scope: CO2 emissions, natural hazards (flood, earthquake, storm, hail, landslide, avalanche), solar potential, building register, noise, radon
limitations: Federal indicative layers only — legally binding maps are cantonal
GEAK energy labels not available via federal API
Swiss coordinate system (LV95) requires transformation for WGS84 input
Some layers have restricted access
citation: swisstopo (2025). geo.admin.ch REST API. https://api3.geo.admin.ch/

name: Cantonal Hazard Maps (Gefahrenkarten)
description: Legally binding cantonal hazard maps for flood, landslide, rockfall, and avalanche via WFS/OGC API. Determines building permits and insurance terms.
longDescription: The Gefahrenkarten service on geodienste.ch aggregates legally binding cantonal natural hazard maps into a nationally harmonized dataset. It shows flood, landslide, rockfall, and avalanche hazard zones with intensity levels (high/medium/low/residual) and return periods. These maps directly determine building permissions, insurance terms, and property values in Switzerland. Available via WMS, WFS, and OGC API Features with coordinate-based spatial queries.
author: Swiss Cantons via geodienste.ch (KGK/CGC)
scenario: Return period hazard zones (intensity × probability matrix)
scope: Flood, landslide, rockfall, avalanche hazard zones with intensity classification
limitations: Coverage varies by canton (not all 26 fully mapped)
Legally binding at cantonal level only
Coordinate system is LV95 (EPSG:2056)
Does not include climate change projections
citation: geodienste.ch (2025). Gefahrenkarten v1.3. https://www.geodienste.ch/services/gefahrenkarten

name: ERM-CH23 Earthquake Risk Model
description: First national earthquake risk model for Switzerland. Estimates building vulnerability and financial losses for 2M+ buildings using OpenQuake.
longDescription: ERM-CH23 is the first comprehensive national earthquake risk model for Switzerland, developed by ETH SED with BAFU and BABS. It combines the SUIhaz2015 seismic hazard model, site amplification effects, and building vulnerability data for 2M+ residential, commercial, and industrial buildings. Implemented in OpenQuake, it calculates financial losses and human impact for earthquake scenarios. An interactive web tool allows individual building risk assessment by location, type, age, and height.
author: ETH SED (Swiss Seismological Service) with BAFU and BABS
scenario: Probabilistic seismic hazard (SUIhaz2015, multiple return periods)
scope: Building damage, financial loss, human impact from earthquake scenarios
limitations: Earthquake-only (no multi-hazard)
Building vulnerability based on typology, not individual inspection
Residential focus — commercial vulnerability less validated
Does not account for secondary effects (fire following earthquake)
citation: ETH SED (2023). Earthquake Risk Model of Switzerland ERM-CH23. http://www.seismo.ethz.ch/

name: Sonnendach.ch
description: Solar energy potential for every roof in Switzerland. Calculates electricity/heat yield, system costs, and payback per building.
longDescription: Sonnendach.ch is the official Swiss tool for assessing solar energy potential, developed by BFE, MeteoSwiss, and swisstopo. It calculates potential electricity and heat yield for every roof surface in Switzerland based on swissBUILDINGS3D geometry and MeteoSwiss radiation data. Outputs include annual kWh production, system sizing, investment costs, and payback period. Companion tool Sonnenfassade.ch covers building facades.
author: BFE (Federal Office of Energy) / MeteoSwiss / swisstopo
scope: Solar electricity and heat potential per roof surface, system sizing, costs, payback
limitations: Roof-only (Sonnenfassade.ch needed for facades)
Based on 3D model geometry — may not reflect recent renovations
Does not account for shading from vegetation or temporary structures
Cost estimates use average Swiss installation prices
citation: BFE (2025). Sonnendach.ch — Solar Energy Potential for Swiss Rooftops. https://www.sonnendach.ch

name: NCCS Climate Scenarios CH2025
description: Official Swiss climate projections on a 1km grid. Temperature, precipitation, and extreme events under multiple warming scenarios. CC BY 4.0.
longDescription: The NCCS Climate Scenarios CH2025 provide localized climate projections for Switzerland at 1km grid resolution, produced by the National Centre for Climate Services with MeteoSwiss and ETH. They include daily temperature, precipitation, and extreme event projections under multiple Global Warming Levels. CH2025 supersedes CH2018 with higher-resolution convection-permitting models. Data is accessible via STAC API.
author: NCCS (National Centre for Climate Services) / MeteoSwiss / ETH
scope: Temperature, precipitation, extreme events (heat, drought, heavy rain) at 1km grid
limitations: Projections have inherent climate model uncertainty
1km grid — not building-level
Does not directly translate to building damage or financial loss
Requires additional modeling to derive property-level risk
citation: NCCS (2025). Swiss Climate Scenarios CH2025. https://www.nccs.admin.ch/

name: Schutz vor Naturgefahren
description: Free address-level natural hazard check for any Swiss building. Combines flood, landslide, hail, avalanche, rockfall, and storm risk with protection recommendations.
longDescription: Schutz vor Naturgefahren is a free public tool by the Prevention Foundation of VKF/AECA (cantonal building insurers). Enter any Swiss address to receive an overlay of all relevant natural hazards from official cantonal hazard maps, plus specific building protection recommendations. Outputs a PDF report covering flood, landslide, hail, earthquake, wind, avalanche, and rockfall exposure.
author: VKF/AECA (Prevention Foundation of Cantonal Building Insurers)
scope: Flood, landslide, hail, earthquake, wind, avalanche, rockfall — with protection measures
limitations: Web tool only — no public API
Swiss addresses only
Outputs are advisory, not legally binding
Does not include climate change projections
citation: VKF/AECA (2025). Schutz vor Naturgefahren. https://www.schutz-vor-naturgefahren.ch

name: KBOB Ökobilanzdaten
description: Authoritative Swiss LCA database for construction materials, building technology, and energy. Used by Minergie, SNBS, and all Swiss green building labels.
longDescription: The KBOB Ökobilanzdaten im Baubereich is the authoritative Swiss lifecycle assessment database for construction, developed by KBOB with ecobau and IPB based on Ecoinvent methodology. Version 8.0 (February 2026) provides primary energy, GHG emissions, and environmental impact data for construction materials, building technology, energy systems, and transport. The database is used by all major Swiss building labels (Minergie, SNBS, BNB) and is accessible via the lcadata.ch API.
author: KBOB / ecobau / IPB (based on Ecoinvent)
scope: Primary energy, GHG emissions, environmental impacts for construction materials, technology, energy, transport
limitations: Swiss-specific data — not directly applicable outside Switzerland
Based on Ecoinvent generic data where Swiss-specific EPDs unavailable
Updated approximately every 2 years
API documentation at lcadata.ch
citation: KBOB (2026). Ökobilanzdaten im Baubereich, Version 8.0. https://www.kbob.admin.ch/

name: PACTA Climate Test Switzerland
description: Swiss government climate alignment test for real estate and mortgage portfolios. Measures building emission intensity against net-zero 2050 pathway.
longDescription: The PACTA Climate Test Switzerland is a biennial climate alignment assessment commissioned by the Swiss Federal Office for the Environment (BAFU) and the State Secretariat for International Financial Matters (SIF). For real estate, it measures the emission intensity of building portfolios against the Swiss net-zero 2050 decarbonization pathway. The real estate module was developed by FPRE/Wüest Partner. Participation is free for Swiss financial institutions. The core PACTA equity/bond software is open source (MIT license on GitHub).
author: BAFU / SIF, developed by RMI + FPRE / Wüest Partner

license.name: Free participation — core software open source (MIT)

scope: Building emission intensity alignment with Swiss decarbonization pathway for real estate and mortgage portfolios
limitations: Biennial test (2020, 2022, 2024, 2026) — not continuous
Real estate module operated by FPRE, not independently downloadable
Portfolio-level assessment, not individual building
Swiss-specific pathway and methodology
citation: BAFU / RMI (2024). PACTA Climate Test Switzerland. https://www.bafu.admin.ch/en/pacta-climate-test

name: SSREI
description: Swiss Sustainable Real Estate Index. 36 indicators across society, economy, and environment for institutional portfolio assessment. GRESB-recognized.
longDescription: SSREI (Swiss Sustainable Real Estate Index) is the leading sustainability assessment framework for Swiss institutional real estate portfolios. It evaluates 36 indicators across society, economy, and environment dimensions, scored 0–3. An automated tool evaluates 23 of 36 indicators using public data. Recognized by GRESB as equivalent to operational green building certification. Verified by SQS.
author: SSREI Association (verified by SQS)
scope: 36 indicators: society, economy, environment for portfolio-level sustainability benchmarking
limitations: Swiss institutional real estate focus
Full assessment requires membership
23/36 indicators automated, 13 require manual input
Portfolio-level, not individual building
citation: SSREI (2024). Swiss Sustainable Real Estate Index. https://ssrei.ch

name: Madaster Switzerland
description: Material passport platform for Swiss buildings. Records all materials from BIM data, calculates circularity index, environmental impact, and residual value.
longDescription: Madaster Switzerland is the Swiss entity of the Madaster Foundation, providing an online registry and platform for material passports. It records all materials and products in a building from BIM (IFC) or Excel data, calculating a circularity index, environmental impact, and residual financial value. Used by SBB for circular construction projects. Enables tracking of material flows for end-of-life reuse and recycling.
author: Madaster Foundation (Dutch origin, Swiss entity madaster.ch)
scope: Material passports, circularity index, embodied environmental impact, residual financial value
limitations: Requires BIM (IFC) or detailed material inventory data
Commercial subscription required
Swiss adoption growing but still niche
Does not assess operational carbon
citation: Madaster (2025). Madaster Switzerland. https://madaster.ch

name: Mobitool
description: Swiss transport CO2 calculator covering all modes. Standardized environmental impact data for commuting, business travel, and freight.
longDescription: Mobitool is a platform for calculating energy consumption and CO2 emissions of all transport modes in Switzerland, developed by SBB, Swisscom, EnergieSchweiz, BAFU, and obu. It provides mobitool factors — standardized environmental impact data covering the full lifecycle of Swiss transport (commuting, business travel, freight). Directly relevant for calculating Scope 3 transport emissions of building locations.
author: SBB / Swisscom / EnergieSchweiz / BAFU / obu
scope: Energy consumption and CO2 emissions for all Swiss transport modes (full lifecycle)
limitations: Swiss-specific transport data and emission factors
Web tool only — no public API
Does not model building-level impacts directly
Requires user input of transport patterns
citation: Mobitool (2025). https://www.mobitool.ch
